They compose naturally: an agent calls the Color API for a palette, the Noise API for a hero texture, and gets back a coherent Tailwind config. The Design Environment (Idea #1) becomes the **orchestration layer** — but it doesn't have to exist for any individual service to ship.\n> **Pricing Model:** Free APIs (maximum adoption, infrastructure-level stickiness) + paid orchestration/dashboard (where switching costs and recurring value live). Same model as Vercel/Supabase.\n> **Market Thesis:** The vibe coding explosion is producing a mass market of non-designer founders shipping AI-generated apps that converge on the same generic aesthetics. They lack vocabulary/tools to differentiate.\n---\n# Architecture Decisions\n**OKLCH is the source of truth for all color.** All tools store and compute in OKLCH internally. Hex/HSL/RGB are derived display/export formats only. Validated building the Harmony Analyzer — hex round-trip introduces \\~0.5–1° hue error from 8-bit quantization. HSL saturation is perceptually misleading. OKLCH chroma is perceptually uniform.\n**APCA over WCAG 2.x.** The `apca-w3` npm package implements the perceptually uniform contrast algorithm replacing WCAG 2.x in WCAG 3. Handles dark mode correctly, factors font size/weight.\n---\n# Ideas Overview\n---\n# Related Existing Projects\n**Chroma Curve** — Monochromatic palette generator using parabolic saturation curves. React 19/TS/Vite/Tailwind v4/Zustand/chroma.js. Separate Claude Project. RANGE slider implements lightness endpoint control.\n**DitherThing** — Dithering/halftone artistic toolkit with grid comparison view. Separate Claude Project. Adjacent to Graindient.\n---\n# Working Prototypes\n- `harmony-analyzer.jsx` — OKLCH color theory harmony validator with three-dimensional scoring\n- `noise-taxonomy.jsx` — 22 procedural noise types, only)</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**EeePC**</td>\n<td>Very old</td>\n<td>Jeep diagnostics, BT OBD2, hotspot</td>\n<td>Pending setup</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Ohmni**</td>\n<td>Pi Zero 2W + setup</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Pi (CRT)**</td>\n<td>Pi 3</td>\n<td>CRT retro video loop</td>\n<td>Pending setup</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Pi (HA)**</td>\n<td>Pi 3/4</td>\n<td>Home Assistant, ESP32 mesh across all machines incl. Backpack when traveling\n- **Spectrum** blocks port forwarding → VPS as public ingress\n- **Caddy** on VPS — reverse proxy + auto SSL, routes subdomains to homelab via Tailscale tunnel\n- **Domain(s)** — subdomains per service (`foundry.x.com`, `vault.x.com`, etc.) — **TODO: purchase domain**\n- **Pi-hole + Unbound** — local DNS + ad blocking, Pi 4 dedicated 24/7\n- Private services (VS Code Server, Obsidian sync, dev tooling) → Tailscale-only, never public\n---\n## Cortex Services\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Service</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Portainer</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Git-backed stacks via Gitea</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>OpenWebUI</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Points at Ollama on AMD Zen5</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Uptime Kuma</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Port 3001</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Speedtest Tracker</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Port 8888 → InfluxDB. LibreSpeed on VPS planned as endpoint</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Frigate</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Port 5000 — NVR with AI object detection</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>WordPress</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Port 32775</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Nu Html Checker (vnu)**</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Port 8889 — W3C HTML/CSS/SVG validator. REST API for CI + Claude validation workflows</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Netbox**</td>\n<td>🔜 Next</td>\n<td>Infrastructure inventory + network diagrams</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Karakeep / Linkwarden**</td>\n<td>🔜 Next</td>\n<td>Bookmark manager — TBD</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Gitea</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Source of truth for compose files + markdown</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Docmost</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Team wiki, real-time collab, [draw.io/Mermaid](http://draw.io/Mermaid)</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>[draw.io](http://draw.io)</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Self-hosted general diagramming</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Foundry VTT</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Tabletop RPG (Crow & Crucible, Mörk Borg)</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Immich</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Self-hosted Google Photos</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Obsidian LiveSync (CouchDB)</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Tailscale only</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>VS Code / code-server</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Tailscale only</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Termix</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Web-based SSH terminal + file editing. MCP</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Local network instance (primary, lower latency)</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Appsmith / Tooljet</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Internal dashboards</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>InfluxDB</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Time series DB (sensors, CPAP, OBD, etc.)</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Grafana</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Unified dashboard</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Prometheus + Node Exporter</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>System metrics all hosts</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Nginx Proxy Manager</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Internal reverse proxy</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>VERT</td>\n<td>🗂 Backlog</td>\n<td>Optional lightweight companion for quick image/doc conversions</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>AnyCable</td>\n<td>🗂 Backlog</td>\n<td>WebSocket with delivery guarantees — revisit at scale</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>ClearFlask</td>\n<td>🗂 Backlog</td>\n<td>Feedback board for Davant products — optional</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n<details>\n<summary>vnu stack compose</summary>\n\t```yaml\nservices:\n  vnu:\n    image: ghcr.io/validator/validator:latest\n    container_name: vnu\n    restart: unless-stopped\n    environment:\n      - CONNECTION_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=15\n      - SOCKET_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=15\n    ports:\n      - \"8889:8888\"\n\t```\n\t**Usage (REST API):**\n\t```bash\ncurl -H \"Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8\" --data-binary @index.html http://optiplex:8889/?out=json\n\t```\n</details>\n---\n## VPS Services\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Service</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Playwright MCP</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>Fallback for remote access</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Caddy</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Reverse proxy + auto SSL</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Vaultwarden</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Bitwarden-compatible password manager</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>[Changedetection.io](http://Changedetection.io)</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Page change monitoring</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>LibreSpeed</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Self-hosted speedtest endpoint for Speedtest Tracker</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>SearXNG</td>\n<td>🗂 Backlog</td>\n<td>Meta-search, mainly for LLM web access</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n---\n## AMD Zen5 Services\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Service</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ollama + OpenWebUI</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td>LLM inference</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Steam server</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td></td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Jellyfin</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Hardware transcode via ROCm/VAAPI</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Barotrauma server</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Can also run on Cortex</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n---\n## Backpack (RTX 4090) Services\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Service</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>ComfyUI</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td></td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Automatic1111</td>\n<td>✅ Installed</td>\n<td></td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n---\n## Workhorse (2080 Super) Services\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Service</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Stable Diffusion</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>After Linux migration</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>OSCAR (CPAP analyzer)</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Desktop app, reads ResMed SD card</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n---\n## Stack Management Pattern\n- All stacks as Docker Compose files in **Gitea** (GitHub for now) → Portainer Git-backed stacks\n- Claude Code pushes compose changes to Git → triggers Portainer API redeploy\n- Source of truth is Git; both human (Portainer UI) and agent (Claude Code) workflows stay in sync\n---\n## Real-time / Multiplayer Stack\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>Use Case</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**Soketi**</td>\n<td>Multiplayer LLM chat, presence, pub/sub</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**y-websocket**</td>\n<td>Davant Studio multiplayer canvas (Yjs CRDT)</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>**AnyCable**</td>\n<td>Stronger delivery guarantees at scale</td>\n<td>🗂 Backlog</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n**Yjs** is the CRDT layer for Davant Studio multiplayer. Pairs with y-websocket on Cortex. Docmost also uses Yjs internally.\n---\n## Collab / Docs Stack\n- **Docmost** — team wiki, real-time editing (Yjs), [draw.io](http://draw.io) + Excalidraw + Mermaid, Spaces per project\n- **Gitea** — compose file source of truth + markdown files\n- **Obsidian** — private vault, stays separate; selectively push content to Docmost\n---\n## Personal Health Data\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Source</td>\n<td>Method</td>\n<td>Destination</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>CPAP (AirSense 11) — daily</td>\n<td>myAir API → HA (resmed_myair_sensors)</td>\n<td>InfluxDB</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>CPAP (AirSense 11) — granular</td>\n<td>SD card → OSCAR (Workhorse)</td>\n<td>Manual</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>openScale</td>\n<td>Bluetooth → Android app → export</td>\n<td>InfluxDB</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ohmni</td>\n<td>BME680 + PMS5003 sensors → WiFi</td>\n<td>InfluxDB</td>\n<td>⬜ Pending build</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\nGoal: correlate AHI, weight, air quality, and environment in a single Grafana dashboard.\n---\n## Security\n### Cortex\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>ClamAV</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Native install, nightly scans, freshclam for auto-updates</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Fail2ban</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Ban IPs on repeated failed SSH/service auth</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>UFW</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Firewall, whitelist only necessary ports</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Unattended-upgrades</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Automatic security patches</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n### VPS (higher priority — fully public-facing)\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>Status</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>ClamAV</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Native install</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Fail2ban</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Especially important here — SSH + Caddy</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>UFW</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Only 80, 443, SSH open</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Unattended-upgrades</td>\n<td>⬜ Planned</td>\n<td>Automatic security patches</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n### General Principles\n- All private services Tailscale-only, never exposed via Caddy\n- Vaultwarden for credential management — no reused passwords\n- Caddy as the only public ingress — narrow attack surface\n- Tailscale ACLs to restrict which machines can talk to which\n---\n## Backlog / TBD\n- [ ] Purchase domain(s) + configure Caddy routing\n- [ ] EeePC Jeep setup (BT OBD2, hotspot, Tailscale)\n- [ ] Ohmni hardware build + hat sewing\n- [ ] MacBook Air → Asahi Linux evaluation\n- [ ] Workhorse Win10 → Linux migration\n- [ ] CRT Pi media playback setup\n- [ ] Lenovo tablet wall mount use case (finalize)\n- [ ] Knip MCP — revisit when dev moves to Cortex\n- [ ] Davant Studio multiplayer (Yjs + y-websocket)\n- [ ] Expose {"metadata":{"type":"page"},"title":"📝 Content Strategy","url":"https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb81bfb357fb265467589d","text":"Here is the result of \"view\" for the Page with URL https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb81bfb357fb265467589d as of 2026-03-27T18:30:56.324Z:\n<page url=\"https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb81bfb357fb265467589d\" icon=\"📝\">\n<ancestor-path>\n<parent-page url=\"https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb81ed81a9c15300cd83f6\" title=\"Fern & Ledger Bookkeeping\"/>\n</ancestor-path>\n<properties>\n{\"title\":\"Content Strategy\"}\n</properties>\n<content>\n## Reader\nSee [Target Client Demographic](https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb814cbb44fe15f12004da) for full profile. Write for these people.\n---\n## Pillars\n**Business Finances 101** - Separating money, financial statements, pricing, cash flow. Builds trust and authority.\n**Tax & Compliance** - Taxes, deductions, business structures, contractors, sales tax. High search volume, real pain points.\n**Industry-Specific** - Deep dives for tattoo, performance arts, makers, witchy & spiritual. Differentiates us from generic firms.\n---\n## Content Architecture\nHub-and-spoke model. Broad content branches into niche-specific content across 4 levels:\n**L1: Pillar Topics** - Broadest. \"Managing money as a creative\" / \"Taxes for creative businesses.\" Ranks for general terms, links down to everything below.\n**L2: Industry Clusters** - Groups of related niches. Body art & modification, performance & entertainment, festival & event services, makers & sellers, witchy & spiritual, salon & beauty. Can be landing pages or section hubs.\n**L3: Specific Roles** - Where real trust gets built. Tattoo shop owner vs tattoo artist (booth renter). Independent face painter vs face painter working for an events company. Tarot reader vs crystal seller. Each has different financial questions.\n**L4: Niche x Topic** - The long tail. \"Quarterly taxes for fire spinners.\" Tiny search volume, massive conversion. Anyone who finds it knows we get them. Can be shorter articles, FAQ posts, or sections within L3 articles.\nStart with L1 (current planned articles), then expand into L3/L4 for priority niches first: witchy & spiritual, tattoo, makers, performance arts.\nFull visual diagram:\n![](https://prod-files-secure.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/b8b4e911-c518-4a77-9fa9-68ab82a14bdc/1a2e7ce1-dd73-4099-9ec2-1be87b5281e1/Untitled-2026-03-27-1125.png?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Content-Sha256=UNSIGNED-PAYLOAD&X-Amz-Credential=ASIAZI2LB466VNS6X2IG%2F20260402%2Fus-west-2%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Date=20260402T080112Z&X-Amz-Expires=3600&X-Amz-Security-Token=IQoJb3JpZ2luX2VjEKD%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2FwEaCXVzLXdlc3QtMiJHMEUCIQDU49GrBVSWz5HKcEAIeZBhmh2y9Bwbjy1sdUYe72zbQAIgKjCUyHC%2BVZsP5cW%2F3ZHqSNQVJN1rqq78KI%2B9YjMkcCQq%2FwMIaRAAGgw2Mzc0MjMxODM4MDUiDNFa65OTKaDALr0WbircA%2FlfaEvGK5lPG09tu%2B0BRSRS%2FThf6oWEzeGQzmF0x6PHYcD1NgHP1fLKB8eW7rMhtbdAFTqxAWOwQhhto%2FGjRV0F%2F%2F6Aj3EYHgz%2FBzhNeT9XFNU10A07aoMwMFHTWR5x747pXdHF8Xgvp0oPvYRuk0BWcB16jm686zQg4Ihb6Jab32iBrvFtBA8siBS61roP%2BiSJIST0%2BekbGwg36x7keRRdnSMYOhGQUodmGSscXi0V4hUvB1X0mA%2FvY6sBZNG1PzoYLSUQml3XRPvB24mhdr9Utuf5c6ckrdvx%2Fa5yTIyyvFfiw%2Fann%2BjFpOdRNLamo7XgfXVX6cYKNalC6OYkRKbE%2F1OTJ5geTHtQqBq3yedepQ%2B23gnZwnUn%2BgPf0mkxTJo8cqYxrz1B3jVhsFrn1ikIEL6iIUltiFEc9jkmR0xiDfc7gVX6I2cuddTlO%2B0mIxV5bfLiD8LY4yEu52l8OM7yt%2F00UBbSLPbco2oRavqbgsfMUzPSkVEgo9A%2Bp2fBMcGXqO%2BFOTkqrlaxQukEjPFkwmpJfMgm%2FdsEZOUevAcXiyBiMdQgot7vAeFKbahXitzKY3wpAJitv%2BEStjee5rUN8j56SUfnWB87Zfz6yl5UVWKGQCKirvPJTWl9MLjCuM4GOqUBRbuksDl8gkoL9p%2FsyYqGhocMKEY2%2FnqVUcSwMhjBPVMgJpCefK95zLAAwvEll3CysqVFQ6tmtm60hFuv6ZflCacj%2BqfOkrvKYKZ3u8KR3Zhb96rIIQ2pkQ4MaMDpF5NO%2FAjAINYz8w8wswiAeT66xEkpMph3WKL%2FeAH6nVc2rfsjX%2FwJd3k8CZKhgnYWd6cdcravMXxUAgdxXRUdfm53iaNUQwPY&X-Amz-Signature=401ff3c5aecef5edcb06ba8853434a6aecc6a9c774125fd84c33469574d73a88&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&x-amz-checksum-mode=ENABLED&x-id=GetObject)\n---\n## Voice & Tone\n- Talk like a smart friend who happens to know about money, not like an accountant\n- Warm, casual, occasionally funny. Never corporate, never stiff\n- Swearing is fine if it fits. \"The IRS does not care that Mercury was in retrograde\" energy\n- Use \"you\" and \"your,\" never \"one should\" or \"business owners may wish to\"\n- Open with a hook that speaks to their lived experience, not a dictionary definition\n- Headlines should feel like something they would actually click, not LinkedIn bait. \"Yes, the IRS Cares About Your Venmo\" \\> \"Understanding Digital Payment Tax Obligations\"\n- Reference their world: gigs, commissions, market days, booth fees, clients who ghost on invoices\n- Metaphors from creative work \\> metaphors from corporate life\n- It is okay to be opinionated. \"Just get a separate bank account. Seriously. Do it today.\"\n---\n## Word & Framing Rules\n- Avoid \"entrepreneur.\" These people are tattoo artists, makers, tarot readers, fire spinners. Call them what they are\n- Avoid \"metaphysical\" as a catch-all. Use the real words: tarot, crystals, spell candles, astrology, energy healing, rituals, sage. Say the actual thing\n- The witchy/spiritual world is normal everyday life for these people. Write about it matter-of-fact, never performative or \"Halloween.\" Crystals have purposes, the zodiac is a casual factor, moon phases matter. Treat it like you have actually been to a witch market, because we have\n- These communities overlap heavily with Burning Man/festival culture, DIY/maker culture, and TTRPG crowds. Know the vibe\n- Never write the way outsiders imagine these people talk. Most of them are just super chill humans running a business\n---\n## Niche Integrity\nEvery niche has a different business model and different financial questions. Never lump two niches into one article just because they seem adjacent. Fire spinners and face painters are not the same. Selling at markets and selling online are not the same. Tattoo shops and salons are not the same. If two groups do not share the same financial situation, they get separate articles. A reader who feels like the article was written specifically for their world will trust us. One who sees their niche lumped in with something vaguely similar will bounce. Specificity is the entire strategy.\n---\n## Anti-patterns\n- No \"In today's economy...\" or \"As a small business owner...\" openers\n- No stock photo energy in the writing. If it sounds like it could be on any generic accounting blog, rewrite it\n- No walls of text. Use short paragraphs, subheadings, and breathing room\n- No passive voice unless it genuinely reads better\n- Never condescend. These people run businesses. They are not dumb, they are just busy doing something they love more than bookkeeping\n- No \"entrepreneur\" in headlines. Ever\n- No corny/cheesy framing of spiritual practices. If you would not say it at a witch market, do not write it\n---\n## Rules\n- Write for humans first, search engines second\n- Frame financial concepts through the lens of creative work\n- Be specific, not generic (\"how fire performers track equipment deductions\" \\> \"tax tips for small businesses\")\n- Every article needs a clear, actionable takeaway\n- Soft CTAs only (\"If this feels like a lot, that is what we are here for\")\n- No jargon without explanation\n- 1,200-2,000 words per article\n---\n## Cadence\nCreate articles in batches (5-7 at a time), then schedule them out at \\~2/month. Batch writing keeps voice and quality consistent. Prioritize P1 articles in the first batch.\n---\n## SEO\nTarget long-tail keywords matching how our audience actually searches: \"tattoo shop bookkeeping,\" \"do I need to collect sales tax on handmade jewelry,\" \"how to pay quarterly taxes as a freelancer.\" Lower competition, higher intent.\n---\n## Key\n**Keystone** = Evergreen content that needs scheduled refreshes (tax law changes, rate updates, new rules). Review every 6-12 months. If it goes stale, it hurts credibility.\n**Purpose: Build Trust** = Financial literacy that shows we know our stuff. Not selling, just helping.\n**Purpose: Drive Traffic** = High search volume topics that bring people to the site organically.\n**Purpose: Convert Leads** = Bottom-of-funnel. Reader is already considering hiring help.\n**Purpose: Niche Authority** = Industry-specific content no other bookkeeping firm writes.\n---\n## Article Tracker\n<database url=\"https://www.notion.so/3998eae24d334fc1ace09d1afab358b7\" inline=\"false\" data-source-url=\"collection://5dad6146-7a54-4187-8654-19c8089b4ec0\">Article Tracker</database>\n<page url=\"https://www.notion.so/330f2be6dbdb81669bb9dc29098305fd\">Content Architecture</page>\n</content>\n</page>"}
```

{"metadata":{"type":"page"},"title":"🛠️ Tooling & Stack","url":"https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8110a8d7ea01c3a2f1d2","text":"Here is the result of \"view\" for the Page with URL https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8110a8d7ea01c3a2f1d2 as of 2026-03-29T06:54:07.538Z:\n<page url=\"https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8110a8d7ea01c3a2f1d2\" icon=\"🛠️\">\n<ancestor-path>\n<parent-page url=\"https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8110898ccb56b09198b2\" title=\"Workshop\"/>\n</ancestor-path>\n<properties>\n{\"title\":\"Tooling & Stack\"}\n</properties>\n<content>\nReference documentation for project tooling, stacks, and best practices.\n---\n## Core Stack\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Layer</td>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Language</td>\n<td>TypeScript</td>\n<td>Always. No plain JS.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>UI Framework</td>\n<td>React 19</td>\n<td>Default for all interactive apps</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Meta Framework</td>\n<td>Astro</td>\n<td>For content/marketing sites. Vite-native.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Styling</td>\n<td>Tailwind CSS v4</td>\n<td>CSS-first `@theme`, Vite plugin, not PostCSS</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>State</td>\n<td>Zustand</td>\n<td>Lightweight, no boilerplate</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Bundler</td>\n<td>Vite</td>\n<td>Default for everything. Fast, ESM-native</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Desktop</td>\n<td>Tauri v2</td>\n<td>For native apps (Davant, OBR). React + Rust.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Color</td>\n<td>chroma.js</td>\n<td>Color manipulation. OKLCH is source of truth.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Primitives</td>\n<td>Radix UI</td>\n<td>Unstyled, accessible. Headless components.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Animation</td>\n<td>Framer Motion (React), GSAP (Astro)</td>\n<td>FM for component transitions, GSAP for scroll/timeline</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Icons</td>\n<td>Phosphor Icons</td>\n<td>Used sparingly alongside text labels</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n## When to Use What\n### New web app (interactive tool)\nReact 19 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind v4 + Zustand\nExamples: Chroma Curve, Harmony Analyzer, future Tailwind toolkit\n### Content/marketing site\nAstro + Tailwind v4 + GSAP\nExamples: Portfolio site, landing pages\n### Desktop app\nTauri v2 + React + TypeScript + Tailwind v4\nExamples: Davant Studio, OBR\n### API / Backend service\nNode.js + TypeScript (or Python for ML-heavy)\nExamples: Color Palette API, NASA API layer\n### Quick prototype / artifact\nSingle-file JSX (Claude artifact) or HTML\nExamples: harmony-analyzer.jsx, noise-taxonomy.jsx, palette-comparison.html\n---\n## Architecture Decisions\n### OKLCH is the source of truth for all color\nAll tools store and compute in OKLCH internally. Hex/HSL/RGB are derived display/export formats only. Every round-trip through hex loses precision to 8-bit quantization (\\~0.5–1° hue error). HSL saturation is perceptually misleading. OKLCH chroma is perceptually uniform.\n**Key utilities** (built in Harmony Analyzer, reusable across ecosystem):\n- `hexToOklch(hex)` — hex string → \\{ L, C, H \\}\n- `oklchToHex(L, C, H)` — OKLCH values → hex string\n### APCA over WCAG 2.x for contrast\nAPCA (`apca-w3` npm) is the candidate replacement for WCAG 3. Perceptually uniform, handles dark mode correctly, factors font size/weight. 47% of WCAG 2 \"passing\" colors are actually unreadable.\n### Design microservices architecture\nEach tool is an independent API/service that's useful standalone but speaks a common language (design tokens in, design tokens out). Free APIs for adoption, paid orchestration/dashboard for revenue. Same model as Vercel/Supabase.\n---\n## Design Principles (from Chroma Curve)\nApplies to all tools in the ecosystem:\n1. **Colors are the content** — UI is a neutral stage\n2. **Dense but legible** — pack info tight, hierarchy through opacity and weight\n3. **Show the math** — expose curves, multipliers, comparisons\n4. **Instrument, not toy** — precise, responsive controls\n5. **Earn trust through consistency** — uniform spacing, predictable patterns\n### Anti-references\n- Coolors/Adobe Color (too playful, consumer-grade)\n- Dashboard/SaaS UI (card-heavy, rounded everything)\n- Academic tools (dry, text-heavy, unapproachable)\n---\n## Key Libraries\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Library</td>\n<td>Use</td>\n<td>npm</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>chroma.js</td>\n<td>Color manipulation & conversion</td>\n<td>chroma-js</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>apca-w3</td>\n<td>APCA contrast algorithm (WCAG 3)</td>\n<td>apca-w3</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>color-thief</td>\n<td>Extract palette from images</td>\n<td>colorthief</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Radix UI</td>\n<td>Accessible headless primitives</td>\n<td>@radix-ui/\\*</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Zustand</td>\n<td>State management</td>\n<td>zustand</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Framer Motion</td>\n<td>React animations</td>\n<td>framer-motion</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>GSAP</td>\n<td>Scroll/timeline animations</td>\n<td>gsap</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n</content>\n</page>"}

Nobody is doing this. Combines Google Fonts Analytics, HTTP Archive BigQuery crawl data, and Typewolf curated lists.\",\"Status\":\"Spark\",\"Vibe\":\"Experiment\",\"url\":\"https://www.notion.so/335f2be6dbdb81fdb2a0f6ee0b648e96\"}\n</properties>\n<content>\n## The Idea\nA dashboard that quantifies font usage trends over time — essentially Google Trends but for fonts. This would let designers and developers see which typefaces are gaining or losing adoption, compare fonts head-to-head, and identify concentration/homogeneity in the font landscape.\n## Why It Matters\nWith vibecoding and AI design tools, the web is converging on the same handful of free typefaces (Inter, Roboto, etc.). A tool that makes this visible would:\n- Quantitatively prove the homogeneity thesis\n- Help designers pick fonts that stand out (low usage, high quality)\n- Track trends in premium/indie font adoption vs. free fonts\n## Gap in the Market\nNo single tool lets you type in two font names and see a clean popularity comparison over time. The pieces exist but nobody has stitched them together.\n## Data Sources\n- **Google Fonts Analytics** — weekly view counts per font family (e.g. Inter: 414B views/year, 57% YoY growth). JS-rendered page, would need scraping or API\n- **HTTP Archive / Web Almanac** — annual crawl of millions of sites, specific font family counts. All queryable on BigQuery (`httparchive.all.*`). Data goes back to 2019\n- **Typewolf** — curated font popularity lists 2013–2026, biased toward designer sites. Good signal for premium/indie font trends\n- **BuiltWith** — tracks font service adoption (Google Fonts vs Adobe vs self-hosted) over time, but service-level not font-level\n- **Fonts In Use** — 33k+ real-world examples, usage count per font, qualitative not quantitative\n## Possible MVP\n1. Pull Google Fonts API data for top 50 fonts, chart concentration over time\n2. Query HTTP Archive BigQuery across 2019–2025 crawls, compute Herfindahl index (market concentration) of font usage\n3. React + Recharts dashboard showing font concentration increasing = proof that vibecoding is homogenizing typography\n4. Add comparison mode: pick any two fonts, see relative usage over time\n## Key Resource: Adobe Fonts via Express\n- **Adobe Express Free plan** — access to \\~4,000 fonts at no cost\n- **Adobe Express Premium (\\~\\$10/month)** — access to all \\~30,000 Adobe Fonts. Likely the cheapest way to get the full Adobe Fonts library (cheaper than a full Creative Cloud subscription)\n- Relevant to the dashboard: Adobe Fonts usage is trackable via BuiltWith and HTTP Archive, making it a good comparison axis against Google Fonts adoption\n## Related Notes\n- Typography as differentiation in the vibecoding era (core thesis)\n- Font foundries & pairing resources (indie alternatives to Google Fonts)\n- Font popularity data sources (detailed breakdown of each data source)\n</content>\n</page>"}

No single tool orchestrates all of these, and certainly nothing exposes it as a unified API. Vibe coders are shipping sites without doing any of this — Claude Code and similar tools don't catch these issues without explicit prompting and direction. Even with prodding, you're still manually orchestrating individual tools across separate terminal sessions.\n## Key Concepts\n- **Single-call audit endpoint**: `POST /audit { url }` → runs all checks, returns structured results with pass/fail/warnings per category\n- **Check categories** (from real workflow pain): Markup & Standards (W3C Nu), Accessibility (axe-core), Performance (Lighthouse), Security (Mozilla Observatory, OWASP ZAP), Links (linkinator), SEO Fundamentals (meta/OG/robots/sitemap/structured data), Favicon Completeness (all sizes + manifest), Asset Checks (image optimization, missing alt text)\n- **MCP server interface**: AI agents (Claude Code, Cursor) can call this directly during development — the missing feedback loop for vibe coders\n- **Self-hostable option**: Many underlying tools are OSS and self-hostable via Docker\n- **Output formats**: JSON (programmatic), HTML report (humans), badge/shield (READMEs)\n- **Vibecoder-friendly output**: Plain language explanations, not just WCAG codes\n## Self-Hostable Tool Inventory\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>Category</td>\n<td>Notes</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>W3C Nu Tailwind</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>axe-core</td>\n<td>A11y</td>\n<td>`@axe-core/cli` or Playwright — industry standard</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>pa11y</td>\n<td>A11y</td>\n<td>CLI, quick one-off checks</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Lighthouse CI</td>\n<td>Perf/A11y/SEO/BP</td>\n<td>Self-hostable via `lhci`, headless</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Unlighthouse</td>\n<td>Site-wide Lighthouse</td>\n<td>Crawls every page automatically</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Mozilla Observatory</td>\n<td>Security headers</td>\n<td>Self-hostable Docker</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>OWASP ZAP</td>\n<td>Security</td>\n<td>Docker, structural linting</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>RealFaviconGenerator</td>\n<td>Favicon analysis</td>\n<td>npm `@realfavicongenerator/check-favicon`  • REST API</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n## Competitive Landscape\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>What It Does</td>\n<td>API?</td>\n<td>Gaps</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>webhint</td>\n<td>Pluggable web linting (a11y, perf, compat, security)</td>\n<td>CLI only</td>\n<td>No favicon, no OG, no single-call API. Broadest competitor but no API/CLI/CI. No HTML validation or favicon</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Screaming Frog</td>\n<td>Desktop crawl + axe-core</td>\n<td>No API</td>\n<td>Desktop app, 500 page limit, not programmable</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>site-audit-seo</td>\n<td>CLI crawler + optional Lighthouse</td>\n<td>CLI only</td>\n<td>SEO-focused. No a11y, security, favicon, HTML validation</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>RealFaviconGenerator</td>\n<td>Favicon check + generation</td>\n<td>Yes (REST + npm)</td>\n<td>Single-purpose (favicon only)</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n## MCP Server Landscape\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>MCP Server</td>\n<td>What It Does</td>\n<td>Categories Covered</td>\n<td>Gaps</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>`@danielsogl/lighthouse-mcp` (npm)</td>\n<td>Most comprehensive Lighthouse MCP. 13+ tools: full audit, perf score, a11y score, SEO analysis, security checks, Core Web Vitals, resource analysis, PWA readiness</td>\n<td>Perf, A11y, SEO, Best Practices, Security (basic), PWA</td>\n<td>Still Lighthouse-only. No HTML validation, no favicon checks, no OG/meta validation, no broken link detection. Security = HTTPS/CSP headers only</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>`lighthouse-mcp` by priyankark (npm)</td>\n<td>Simpler Lighthouse wrapper. Audit + perf score tools</td>\n<td>Perf, A11y, SEO, Best Practices</td>\n<td>Fewer tools than danielsogl. Same Lighthouse-only limitation</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>`@apixies/mcp-server` (npm, 54+ tools)</td>\n<td>Utility toolbox: SSL checker, security headers, DNS lookup, link checker, meta tag extractor, robots.txt parser, favicon fetcher, screenshot, plus converters/generators</td>\n<td>Security (SSL/headers), Links, Meta tags, Favicon (fetch only), DNS, Infra</td>\n<td>Atomic tools — no orchestration or unified report. No a11y (no axe-core), no Lighthouse/perf, no HTML validation. Favicon fetch ≠ favicon audit. Free tier 75 req/day</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>`broken-link-checker-mcp` by davinoishi</td>\n<td>Single-page or full-site broken link checking</td>\n<td>Broken links only</td>\n<td>Single-purpose</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>`deadlink-checker-mcp`</td>\n<td>Dead link checker with sitemap support</td>\n<td>Broken links only</td>\n<td>Single-purpose, newer alternative</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Apify MCP Server (gateway)</td>\n<td>Access to 8000+ Apify actors including Lighthouse audit, SEO audit, favicon validator actors</td>\n<td>Varies by actor</td>\n<td>Platform-dependent (requires Apify account/token). Actors are separate — no unified prelaunch workflow. Pay-per-use</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n**MCP Takeaway:** The space has pieces but no unified prelaunch server. `danielsogl/lighthouse-mcp` covers Lighthouse categories well. Apixies covers infra-level checks (SSL, headers, DNS, links). But nobody combines them with HTML validation, favicon completeness, and OG/meta checking into a single orchestrated \"prelaunch audit\" MCP server. **The MCP opportunity is confirmed open.**\n## Key Takeaway\n**The white space is clear across both APIs and MCP.** Every existing tool either: (a) covers only one category deeply, (b) is SEO-focused enterprise SaaS, or (c) is GUI/SaaS with no API. In MCP-land, Lighthouse wrappers and utility toolboxes exist but nobody orchestrates the full prelaunch checklist. **A single MCP server that runs HTML validation + a11y + performance + security + favicon + OG/meta + broken links and returns one unified pass/fail report has no incumbent.**\n## Open Questions\n- Scope of v0: minimum checks that make this feel unified vs. wrapper? Probably HTML validation + a11y + OG/meta + favicons + security headers + broken links + Lighthouse\n- Hosted vs. self-host first? Underlying tools need headless Chrome — hosting cost non-trivial. Self-host Docker image might be v0 play\n- Pricing: Free tier + paid CI/CD? Free API + paid MCP? Free checks + paid reports?\n- Relationship to VibeCheckt: cosmetic quality vs. production readiness — complementary, could share infrastructure\n- Name: LaunchReady? ShipCheck? PreflightAPI?\n- Claude Code integration: MCP server that runs automatically as final step before \"your site is ready\"\n</content>\n</page>"}

{"metadata":{"type":"page"},"title":"🏗️ Heading Auditor — Browser Extension","url":"https://www.notion.so/335f2be6dbdb81a39948d36dc4dbfa25","text":"Here is the result of \"view\" for the Page with URL https://www.notion.so/335f2be6dbdb81a39948d36dc4dbfa25 as of 2026-04-01T20:22:15.057Z:\n<page url=\"https://www.notion.so/335f2be6dbdb81a39948d36dc4dbfa25\" icon=\"🏗️\">\n<ancestor-path>\n<parent-data-source url=\"collection://85de4634-6cbe-4827-85f8-423e8732abb8\" name=\"Ideas\"/>\n<ancestor-2-database url=\"https://www.notion.so/7f092a4ec6ad46d993045ae20ec6b3c0\" title=\"Ideas\"/>\n<ancestor-3-page url=\"https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8187be8afd5e5378ffc4\" title=\"Product Ideas\"/>\n<ancestor-4-page url=\"https://www.notion.so/332f2be6dbdb8110898ccb56b09198b2\" title=\"Workshop\"/>\n</ancestor-path>\n<properties>\n{\"Category\":[\"Dev Tool\"],\"Connects To\":\"#11 (UX Audit Tool)\",\"Demand Validated\":\"__YES__\",\"Has Prototype\":\"__NO__\",\"Idea\":\"Heading Auditor — Browser Extension\",\"Idea ID\":\"IDEA-13\",\"Last Updated\":\"2026-04-01T20:22:15.002Z\",\"Priority\":\"Next Up\",\"Ship Signal\":\"Strong\",\"Status\":\"Concept\",\"url\":\"https://www.notion.so/335f2be6dbdb81a39948d36dc4dbfa25\"}\n</properties>\n<content>\n## Core Problem\nNo browser extension does heading structure validation well. HeadingsMap (the main option on Firefox/Chrome) has poor UX — low contrast UI that fails its own WCAG checks, mystery icons with no clear purpose, only flags the first skipped heading level instead of cascading errors, and its page overlays are just small corner badges with no bounding box outlines.\nDevelopers and accessibility auditors need to quickly see heading hierarchy errors **on the page itself** in a way that survives screenshots for documentation and audits.\n## Key Concepts\n- **Chrome Manifest V3 extension** with Side Panel API for the tree view + content script for page overlays\n- **Cascade error propagation**: every heading under an invalid parent gets flagged, not just the first skip (e.g. `h2 → h4, h4, h5` flags all three, not just the first h4)\n- **Page overlay outlines**: colored `box-shadow` borders around each heading's bounding box with level badge labels (H1, H2, etc.) — green for valid, red for errors\n- **Side panel tree**: indented, collapsible heading tree with level numbers, error annotations, stats bar, and click-to-scroll to page heading\n- **Screenshot-friendly**: overlays are injected DOM (not extension chrome) so they show up in any full-page screenshot tool. Side panel is native Chrome Side Panel so it stays out of the capture\n- Error labels on overlays (\"Skipped H3\") visible directly on page\n## Architecture\n- **Side Panel** (React) — heading tree, toggle controls, stats. Communicates with content script via `chrome.runtime.sendMessage`\n- **Content Script** — scans `h1-h6` + `[role=\"heading\"]`, injects overlay divs (`position: absolute`, `pointer-events: none`, `box-shadow` borders + badge labels), handles scroll-to and highlight on message from side panel\n- **Popup** — minimal, just opens side panel + toggle overlay visibility\n- `ResizeObserver` + `MutationObserver` for reflow/DOM change handling\n- No background worker needed for v1\n## Validation Algorithm\nWalk headings in DOM order, maintaining a stack of valid ancestor levels. For each heading, check if level ≤ lastLevel + 1. If it skips, flag it AND flag every subsequent heading that remains in the skipped subtree until a heading resets to a valid ancestor level.\n```javascript\nh1        ✓  (stack: [1])\n  h2      ✓  (stack: [1,2])\n    h4    ✗  (skipped 3)\n    h4    ✗  (still no 3)\n    h5    ✗  (child of invalid subtree)\n  h2      ✓  (back to valid level)\n    h3    ✓  (stack: [1,2,3])\n```\n## Competitive Landscape\n<table header-row=\"true\">\n<tr>\n<td>Tool</td>\n<td>What It Does</td>\n<td>Gaps</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>HeadingsMap (Firefox/Chrome)</td>\n<td>Sidebar tree of headings + optional page badges</td>\n<td>Only flags first skipped level, no error cascading. No bounding box outlines. UI has poor contrast (fails WCAG AA itself). Mystery icons.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>WAVE (WebAIM)</td>\n<td>Full a11y audit with heading structure in Structure tab</td>\n<td>Injects icons into page DOM (can mess up layout/screenshots). Heading view is buried in a broader audit tool, not focused.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Accessibility Insights (Microsoft)</td>\n<td>FastPass + guided assessment with heading checks</td>\n<td>Lives in DevTools, not visible in screenshots. Heading structure is part of assessment flow, not a standalone view.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Silktide Toolbar</td>\n<td>200+ checks including heading hierarchy</td>\n<td>Heading checker flags duplicate H1s but doesn't do cascade validation. Broader tool, not heading-focused.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Chrome DevTools A11y Tree</td>\n<td>Full accessibility tree in Elements panel</td>\n<td>Not an overlay, requires DevTools open. No error highlighting or heading-specific filtering.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>DigitalA11Y Tublets</td>\n<td>Visual overlays for headings, landmarks, forms</td>\n<td>Audit-focused overlays, not heading-error-specific. No tree sidebar.</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n**Key Takeaway**: Every existing tool either buries heading validation inside a broader audit suite, or shows structure without proper error detection. None combine cascade error propagation + page overlay outlines + a clean dedicated tree view. The screenshot use case is completely unserved — no tool produces heading annotations that look good in a full-page capture.\n## Match HeadingsMap\n- Heading tree with level numbers and indentation\n- Collapsible tree nodes\n- Page overlays (badges on headings)\n- Hidden headings count\n- Click-to-scroll from tree to heading\n## Improve on HeadingsMap\n- Full cascade error propagation, not just first skip\n- Box outlines around headings (not just corner badges)\n- Red/green color coding in both tree AND overlays\n- Clean, high-contrast side panel UI that actually passes WCAG\n- Clear, labeled controls instead of mystery icons\n## v2 Backlog\n- Landmarks tab\n- ARIA `role=\"heading\"` + `aria-level` support\n- Export heading structure as markdown/JSON\n- Keyboard shortcuts for navigating between errors\n## Open Questions\n- Should error overlay labels (\"Skipped H3\") always be visible or only on hover?\n- Support Firefox via browser polyfill, or Chrome-only v1?\n- Should hidden headings (`display:none`, `visibility:hidden`, `aria-hidden`) be shown in tree with a visual indicator, or filtered to a separate section?\n</content>\n</page>"}
```

II Product Page\nA product page that feels more like a campaign than a spec sheet. The \"K.O.\" name is taken literally with a full-bleed boxing hero image, and the entire page leans into that energy.\n### What stands out\nThe hero section uses a classic boxing photograph as the backdrop, with the K.O. II logotype rendered as large outlined letterforms overlaid on top. Orange accent text (Japanese katakana) anchors the bottom left. The effect is dramatic and conceptual rather than purely functional.\nBelow the hero, a scrolling ticker bar repeats the price and CTA, acting as both a divider and a persistent nudge.\nThe product detail section below is a total contrast shift: clean white background, straight-on product photography, minimal text. Then the layout punches back with bold orange badge-style headlines (\"NO. 1 CHAMPION\") on white.\nThe page rhythm alternates between high-energy visual moments and clean breathing room. Typography and color do all the work with almost no traditional UI elements.\n</content>\n</page>"}

No designer on the team, don't know what they don't know. The manual audit pipeline works but takes hours.\n## Proof of Concept\nBuilt a full working pipeline manually: Claude Code + Impeccable plugin → Nielsen's heuristics evaluation → annotated screenshot report → polished 8-page \"Spectral Cartography\" PDF with radar chart, numbered callouts, persona analysis, cognitive load assessment, prioritized action plan. The output is genuinely impressive.\n## Key Concepts\n- **Automated Audit Pipeline** — point at URL, AI evaluates against Nielsen's heuristics, generates findings with severity ratings\n- **Live Site Annotations** — inject findings as overlay on live site. Numbered callouts pinned to DOM elements, color-coded by severity\n- **Review Workflow** — sign off, reject, or add notes to each annotation. Status persists across runs.\n- **Scheduled / CI Integration** — run on every deploy. Track UX score over time. Regression detection.\n- **Scoring Dashboard** — Nielsen's heuristics spider chart, cognitive load, WCAG compliance\n## Competitive Gap\nTwo separate worlds that nobody has combined: annotation tools (BugHerd, Ruttl, Volley) for humans to pin comments, and audit tools (Lighthouse) for automated scoring. Nobody does AI audit + live annotations + review workflow. Lighthouse scores technical quality, not design quality.\n### Competitor Deep Dive: Approval Studio\n[approval.studio](http://approval.studio) — Online proofing software for creative teams. Directly relevant as the most feature-complete annotation/review tool in this space.\n**What they do well:**\n- 5 annotation shapes pinned to design mockups with threaded comments\n- 4 automated compare modes to diff between iterations (their key differentiator)\n- External review via shareable links — no account needed for reviewers\n- Live chat-style discussions inside the review tool (real-time, no refresh)\n- Full project management layer: task assignments, workflows, reporting, project timeline\n- Integrations: Adobe CC extension, Shopify plugin, Zapier, Slack, GraphQL + REST APIs, MCP server\n- Barcode reader, on-screen ruler, color separation tools (packaging design focus)\n- Mobile app (iOS)\n**Pricing:** Lite \\$12/user/mo (5 users, 5GB), Pro \\$32/user/mo, Pro XL \\$20/user/mo (15 users, 30GB). 14-day free trial.\n**Where we differentiate:**\n- They're built for designers reviewing static assets (images, PDFs, packaging). We're built for **live sites** — DOM-aware annotations, not pixel pins.\n- Zero AI audit capability. All annotations are manual/human.\n- No automated scoring, heuristics evaluation, or regression detection.\n- Target market is design agencies/packaging teams, not solo devs or vibe coders.\n- No CI/deploy integration. Their workflow is upload → review → approve, not continuous.\n</content>\n</page>"}

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 08:11:42Z)**_

Good question. The memory system at `/Users/grant/.claude/projects/-Users-grant-code-nasa-apod/memory/` is **project-scoped**, so it won't be available in other project conversations.

For truly global access, two options:

1. **Add a reference to your global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`** - This is loaded in every conversation, every project. A compact map there would always be available. Downside: it adds bulk to an instructions file.

2. **Save the map as a standalone file somewhere global** (e.g., `~/.claude/references/notion-map.md`) and add a one-liner in your global CLAUDE.md pointing to it. Keeps instructions clean, map is fetchable when needed.

I'd recommend **option 2** - keep the global CLAUDE.md lean and just add a pointer:

```
## References
- Notion workspace map: ~/.claude/references/notion-map.md
```

Then the full hierarchy lives in its own file that any conversation can read on demand.

Want me to set it up that way?

_**User (2026-04-02 08:14:17Z)**_

how do we ensure it stays updated as claude code makes changes to notion?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 08:14:28Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 08:14:33Z)**_

Good question. A few approaches, from simplest to most automatic:

1. **Instruction in global CLAUDE.md** — Add a rule like "after creating/moving/deleting Notion pages, update `~/.claude/references/notion-map.md`." Simple, relies on Claude following instructions. Works most of the time.

2. **PostToolUse hook** — A hook that fires after `notion-create-pages`, `notion-move-pages`, or `notion-update-page` calls and injects a reminder to update the map. More reliable than just an instruction since it's event-driven.

3. **On-demand skill** — A `/notion-map` slash command that re-crawls the workspace and rebuilds the file from scratch. Good for periodic refresh or after big reorganizations.

My recommendation: **#1 + #3**. The instruction catches day-to-day changes cheaply, and the skill handles drift or big restructures. Hooks (#2) would fire on every Notion edit (including content-only changes that don't affect structure), which would be noisy.

Want me to set up both?

1	---
2	name: frontend-slides
3	description: Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Helps non-designers discover their aesthetic through visual exploration rather than abstract choices.
4	---
5	
6	# Frontend Slides
7	
8	Create zero-dependency, animation-rich HTML presentations that run entirely in the browser.
9	
10	## Core Principles
11	
12	1. **Zero Dependencies** — Single HTML files with inline CSS/JS. No npm, no build tools.
13	2. **Show, Don't Tell** — Generate visual previews, not abstract choices. People discover what they want by seeing it.
14	3. **Distinctive Design** — No generic "AI slop." Every presentation must feel custom-crafted.
15	4. **Viewport Fitting (NON-NEGOTIABLE)** — Every slide MUST fit exactly within 100vh. No scrolling within slides, ever. Content overflows? Split into multiple slides.
16	
17	## Design Aesthetics
18	
19	You tend to converge toward generic, "on distribution" outputs. In frontend design, this creates what users call the "AI slop" aesthetic. Avoid this: make creative, distinctive frontends that surprise and delight.
20
```
</details></tool-use>

---
name: notion-map
description: Re-crawl the Notion workspace and rebuild ~/.claude/references/notion-map.md with the current hierarchy. Run when the user says "update notion map", "refresh notion map", "rebuild notion map", or after major Notion restructuring.
---

# Notion Map

Rebuild the Notion workspace map at `~/.claude/references/notion-map.md` by crawling the workspace via Notion MCP tools.

## Process

1. **Search for all pages** using `notion-search` with broad queries to surface as many pages as possible (try multiple queries like "page", "project", common words, etc.)

2. **Fetch each discovered page** using `notion-fetch` to read its `ancestor-path`, which reveals parent-child relationships. Fetch in parallel batches for speed.

3. **Identify parent pages** that appear in ancestor paths but weren't in search results. Fetch those too to find their children.

4. **Repeat until no new parents are discovered** — the tree is complete when every ancestor-path reference has been visited.

5. **Rebuild the map file** at `~/.claude/references/notion-map.md` with:
   - The full hierarchy as an indented tree
   - Notion UUIDs in parentheses for each page/database
   - `[database]` labels for database entries
   - A "Last updated" date
   - A Notes section explaining key organizational concepts

## Output Format

Follow the existing format in `~/.claude/references/notion-map.md`. Preserve the structure:

\```markdown
# Notion Workspace Map

**Workspace:** Grant Keller's Notion HQ
**Last updated:** YYYY-MM-DD

## Hierarchy

(indented tree with UUIDs)

## Notes

(contextual notes about the workspace organization)
\```

## Important

- Use parallel `notion-fetch` calls to minimize crawl time
- Only update the map file — do not create/modify/delete any Notion content
- If a page returns a 404 or permission error, note it as inaccessible and skip
- Keep UUIDs in the map — they're used by other tools to navigate directly to pages

```

732	          rel="noopener noreferrer">Grant Keller</a> at <a href="https://davantsystems.com"
733	          class="animated-bottom-border" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Davant Systems</a></p>

736	          Astronomy Picture of the Day</a>

745	  <script>
746	    const PROD_BASE = 'https://api.nasapicture.com';
747	    const API_BASE = location.hostname === 'localhost' ? `http://localhost:8787` : PROD_BASE;
748	    const img = document.getElementById('hero-img');
749	
750	    function onImageReady() { img.classList.add('loaded'); }
751	
752	    const defined_replace = (str) => str.replace(PROD_BASE, API_BASE);
753	    img.src = defined_replace(img.src);
754	    document.querySelectorAll(`a[href^="${PROD_BASE}"]`).forEach(a => {
755	      a.href = defined_replace(a.href);
756	    });
757	    document.querySelectorAll('.usage-url[data-copy]').forEach(el => {
758	      el.dataset.copy = defined_replace(el.dataset.copy);
759	    });
760	    document.querySelectorAll('.usage-url').forEach(el => {
761	      el.childNodes.forEach(node => {
762	        if (node.nodeType === Node.TEXT_NODE) {
763	          node.textContent = defined_replace(node.textContent);
764	        }
765	      });
766	    });
767	
768	    // Fetch APOD metadata for caption (image loads directly from proxy)
769	    fetch(`${API_BASE}/info`)
770	      .then(r => r.json())
771	      .then(data => {
772	        if (data.title) {
773	          img.alt = data.title;
774	          document.getElementById('caption-title').textContent = data.title;
775	          const dateEl = document.getElementById('caption-date');
776	          dateEl.textContent = data.date;
777	          dateEl.setAttribute('datetime', data.date);
778	          dateEl.title = 'NASA publishes on Eastern Time';
779	          document.getElementById('hero-caption').classList.add('visible');
780	        }
781	      })
782	      .catch(() => { });
783	
784	    img.addEventListener('load', onImageReady);
785	
786	    // Copy on click
787	    document.querySelectorAll('.usage-url[data-copy]').forEach(el => {
788	      el.addEventListener('click', () => {
789	        const text = el.dataset.copy;
790	        navigator.clipboard.writeText(text).then(() => {
791	          el.classList.add('copied');
792	          el.querySelector('.copy-btn').classList.add('copied');
793	          setTimeout(() => {
794	            el.classList.remove('copied');
795	            el.querySelector('.copy-btn').classList.remove('copied');
796	          }, 2000);
797	        });
798	      });
799	    });
800	
801	    // Scroll fade-in
802	    const observer = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {
803	      entries.forEach(entry => {
804	        if (entry.isIntersecting) {
805	          entry.target.classList.add('visible');
806	          observer.unobserve(entry.target);
807	        }
808	      });
809	    }, { threshold: 0.15 });
810	
811	    document.querySelectorAll('.fade-up').forEach(el => observer.observe(el));
812	
813	    // Focus mode
814	    const toggle = document.getElementById('focus-toggle');
815	    const overlay = document.getElementById('focus-overlay');
816	    function setFocusMode(on) {
817	      document.body.classList.toggle('focus-mode', on);
818	    }
819	
820	    // Toggle focus mode on button click
821	    toggle.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
822	      e.stopPropagation();
823	      setFocusMode(!document.body.classList.contains('focus-mode'));
824	    });
825	
826	    // Exit focus mode on overlay click or Escape key
827	    overlay.addEventListener('click', () => setFocusMode(false));
828	    document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
829	      if (e.key === 'Escape') setFocusMode(false);
830	    });
831	  </script>
832	</body>
833	
834	</html>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 10:40:05Z)**_

I see the issue. The focus overlay is at `z-index: 50`, the hero image is at `z-index: 60`, but the focus toggle button is in the header which has no explicit `z-index`. So when the overlay appears, the toggle gets darkened along with everything else.

The fix: give the `.focus-toggle` a `z-index` higher than the overlay. But since it's inside `.site-header`, the header needs to establish a stacking context that's above the overlay too. The cleanest approach is to put the header above the overlay when in focus mode.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 10:40:08Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 10:40:13Z)**_

This removes the always-on `z-index: 60` from the toggle (which wasn't working anyway since the header didn't create a stacking context), and instead lifts `.site-nav` above the overlay (`z-index: 50`) only when focus mode is active. The toggle stays clickable and unaffected by the darkening overlay, while everything else (logo, tagline, etc.) gets dimmed.

The problem is that `.site-nav` is inside `.site-header`, and the header doesn't have a stacking context, so the child `z-index` gets ignored. The header itself needs to be above the overlay.

Wait, that would put the entire header (logo, tagline) above the overlay too. You only want the toggle. Let me rethink.

The real fix: pull the toggle out of the header's stacking context isn't clean. Instead, give the header a stacking context that's above the overlay, but visually hide the other header elements in focus mode.

Actually, the simplest approach: just make the toggle `position: fixed` in focus mode so it escapes the header's flow entirely.

1	---

6	
7	# Add Vite for SCSS and HMR
8	
9	The landing page (`landing/index.html`) is currently a single HTML file with all styles inlined, served in dev via `npx serve`. This works but has two pain points:
10	
11	1. **No HMR** — every change requires a manual browser refresh
12	2. **No SCSS** — styles are plain CSS inlined in a `<style>` tag, making them harder to organize as the page grows
13	
14	## What this would change
15	
16	- Add Vite as a dev dependency for the landing page
17	- Extract inline styles into one or more `.scss` files imported by the HTML
18	- Replace `npx serve landing` in the `dev` / `dev:landing` scripts with `vite` (dev server with HMR)
19	- Add a `build` step that outputs a static `dist/` for production deployment
20	- Vite's built-in PostCSS pipeline can also handle Tailwind if that's added later
21	
22	## Open questions
23	
24	- Should Vite live at the project root or in a `landing/` sub-package with its own `vite.config.ts`?
25	- Does the production landing page stay on Cloudflare Pages, or continue deploying as static files alongside the Worker?
26	- Worth adding Tailwind at the same time, or keep it pure SCSS for now?
27
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 12:48:31Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 12:48:39Z)**_

Good calls. A few responses:

**Vite in `/landing`** — Yes, that's the right move. The Worker and landing page are separate concerns with separate deploy targets. A `landing/vite.config.ts` + its own `package.json` keeps them isolated. The root `package.json` stays focused on the Worker.

**Cloudflare Pages** — The note was asking about deployment strategy. Cloudflare Pages is a separate product from Workers, purpose-built for static sites (auto-builds from git, preview deploys, etc.). Right now your landing page is just a static HTML file. The question is: after adding Vite with a build step, do you deploy the built output via Cloudflare Pages (separate from the Worker), or keep deploying it however it's deployed now? How is `nasapicture.com` currently served?

**CSS** — Noted, no Tailwind. One clarification though: do you want to stick with plain CSS (just extracted from the inline `<style>` into `.css` files), or use SCSS as the note suggested? Vite handles both natively. SCSS would give you nesting, variables (beyond CSS custom properties), and partials for organization, but your current styles are already well-organized with CSS custom properties so plain CSS might be enough.

----
-type: note
-status: inbox
-created: 2026-04-01
----
-
-# Add Vite for SCSS and HMR
-
-The landing page (`landing/index.html`) is currently a single HTML file with all styles inlined, served in dev via `npx serve`. This works but has two pain points:
-
-1. **No HMR** — every change requires a manual browser refresh
-2. **No SCSS** — styles are plain CSS inlined in a `<style>` tag, making them harder to organize as the page grows
-
-## What this would change
-
-- Add Vite as a dev dependency for the landing page
-- Extract inline styles into one or more `.scss` files imported by the HTML
-- Replace `npx serve landing` in the `dev` / `dev:landing` scripts with `vite` (dev server with HMR)
-- Add a `build` step that outputs a static `dist/` for production deployment
-- Vite's built-in PostCSS pipeline can also handle Tailwind if that's added later
-
-## Open questions
-
-- Should Vite live at the project root or in a `landing/` sub-package with its own `vite.config.ts`?
-- Does the production landing page stay on Cloudflare Pages, or continue deploying as static files alongside the Worker?
-- Worth adding Tailwind at the same time, or keep it pure SCSS for now?
- Current branch: dev
- Recent commits: c87edc2 fix(landing): keep focus toggle above overlay in focus mode

c26cfb8 fix: allow HEAD requests and use SD image for OG metadata
